context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Civil Society: NGOs and Social Movements

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
There is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development. How good are NGOs as development agents? In what circumstances are they more effective as agents of development? What are their limitations? What is the role of other civil society actors in development such as social movements? What is the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m stuck on this reflection about NGOs and social movements in development. I kind of understand the topic, but it all feels too broad, and I’m not sure how to turn the prompt into a focused 700–800 word piece.
exchanges.tutor: That's a common feeling with broad prompts! Let's start simple: of all the questions the prompt raises — NGO effectiveness, their limitations, social movements, civil society boundaries — which one feels most interesting or provocative to you right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very start and feeling overwhelmed by the breadth of the prompt. Rather than scaffolding the structure for them, I should ask a focusing question that helps them identify what they already know and care about, which will naturally narrow the scope. This is a guided discovery move — get them to articulate their own starting point before any structure is imposed.

exchanges.student: Honestly, I keep wanting to answer all of them at once, which is probably the problem. Maybe the most interesting part is whether NGOs are actually good development agents, but then I immediately get lost because I feel like the answer is just “sometimes,” and that seems too vague.
exchanges.tutor: "Sometimes" is actually a strong analytical starting point — the interesting question is what makes the difference. What conditions or circumstances do you think determine whether an NGO is effective or not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a good instinct — that 'sometimes' is the core of their answer — but is dismissing it as too vague. This is actually a productive starting point for a nuanced argument. I should push them to see that 'sometimes' is not a weakness but the beginning of an analytical claim, by asking them what conditions make the difference. This moves them toward the 'in what circumstances' part of the prompt without me imposing structure.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure, and I think that’s where I freeze. Could you maybe just explain the whole NGO debate in development pretty broadly first, so I can see the big picture before I try to narrow it down?
exchanges.tutor: That's not quite my role here — I'm here to help you develop your own thinking, not to summarize the field for you. You likely have readings and course materials on this. What do they say about NGO effectiveness? Even one idea from your readings would be a good starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to lecture them on the topic, which would bypass the learning process and violate the 'never give the answer directly' rule. I should redirect them back to their own thinking and the readings they should have done for the course. A gentle but firm refusal is appropriate here, followed by a question that gets them back on track.

exchanges.student: That didn’t really help me unfreeze, so I’m trying a different angle. I remember a general idea that NGOs can be closer to local communities than the state, but I also remember criticism that they can be donor-driven and not very accountable, and I don’t know how to connect that into an actual reflection.
exchanges.tutor: Those two ideas are actually in direct tension with each other — and that tension is the core of a strong reflection. Think about it: if NGOs are close to communities, but accountable to donors, who are they really serving? What does that imply about when they work well versus when they don't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually surfaced two substantive and opposing ideas from their readings — local proximity as a strength, and donor-driven accountability as a weakness. This is good progress and I should affirm it briefly. Now I should push them to see that these two ideas are already in tension with each other, and that tension is exactly what makes a reflection interesting. A single question can help them see the connection without me making it for them.

exchanges.student: I guess it implies they work better when donor goals and local needs line up, and worse when funding pressures distort priorities. But now I feel like I need a big explanation of NGOs versus the state versus social movements, because I’m not sure where civil society starts and ends.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a sharp observation — hold onto it, it could be the spine of your whole piece. Before expanding to the state and social movements, can you think of a concrete example, even a rough one, that illustrates what happens when donor priorities and local needs diverge?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely good analytical point about alignment of donor goals and local needs — I should briefly affirm this. However, they are now trying to expand the scope again into a broad conceptual overview, which risks the same paralysis they started with. I should affirm their insight and redirect them: that insight is already a thesis-level claim. Rather than letting them drift into conceptual territory, I should push them to stay with what they have and build on it.

exchanges.student: I can’t think of a solid example, and I’m worried I’ll make one up badly. Could you just give me a broad comparison of NGOs, the state, and social movements in development so I have something clearer to work from?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I'll do — your readings are the right place to look for examples. For a reflection piece, a rough or partial example from your course materials is completely fine. What cases or contexts have come up in your readings or class discussions, even briefly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide content for them, which I must refuse. However, they have a legitimate concern about not having a concrete example. I should redirect them to their course materials rather than providing the comparison myself. I should also reassure them that a rough example is fine for a reflection piece — perfectionism about examples is holding them back.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on specific cases, so maybe I should stay abstract. I think my point is basically that NGOs can deliver services and advocate, but they can’t replace politics or the state, and social movements seem more political and disruptive. I’m still not sure if that’s too generic.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid analytical claim, not generic at all. You now have: a tension (donor accountability vs. local needs), a condition for effectiveness (alignment of goals), and a distinction between NGOs and social movements (service/advocacy vs. political disruption). That's enough to start writing. What would your opening argument be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now articulated a genuinely substantive claim — NGOs can deliver and advocate but can't replace politics, and social movements are more political. This is real analytical progress and not too generic at all. I should affirm this briefly and push them to sharpen it into a more precise claim rather than letting them second-guess themselves. We're also getting close to having enough material to start writing, so I should nudge them toward a draft.

exchanges.student: I’m tempted to say NGOs are useful but limited development agents: they’re most effective when they’re embedded in local contexts and complement public institutions, but weaker when they become donor-driven substitutes for politics or the state. I’m not sure if that’s enough, though, and I still kind of want a fuller explanation before I commit.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong thesis — commit to it. The fuller explanation you're looking for will emerge as you write. One thing to keep in mind: your prompt also asks about social movements and civil society boundaries specifically. How does your thesis connect to those parts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong thesis-level claim. I should affirm it clearly and push back on their hesitation to commit — the desire for 'a fuller explanation first' is a procrastination pattern at this point. They have enough to write. I should encourage them to commit to this thesis and start drafting, while noting they can refine as they go. I should also briefly signal that their piece still needs to address social movements and civil society boundaries, as the prompt requires.

exchanges.student: Maybe social movements matter because they can pressure the state and reshape what development even means, instead of just delivering projects. I’m still fuzzy on the “boundary of civil society” part though, and I feel like I’m missing the big picture there.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's a meaningful distinction between NGOs and social movements. On civil society boundaries: think about it this way — does every community group or informal network count as civil society, or does context matter? You don't need to resolve this fully before writing. You now have a thesis, a distinction between NGOs and social movements, and a loose thread on boundaries. It's time to start a draft — what's your first paragraph?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made another good analytical point about social movements and pressure on the state. The 'boundary of civil society' confusion is legitimate but risks becoming another rabbit hole that delays writing. We've now had several exchanges building up good analytical material. I should affirm their social movements point, give a minimal nudge on civil society boundaries, and firmly redirect them toward actually drafting — they have more than enough to start.

exchanges.student: I’m still hesitating because I feel like if I start drafting now, I’ll just repeat clichés. Could you at least give me one compact model paragraph or a more complete overview of the “boundary of civil society” idea so I can imitate the structure?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write a model paragraph for you — that's your job, and imitating my writing wouldn't help you learn. The fear of clichés is actually a good instinct; let it push you to use your own specific claims rather than generic ones. You already have original ideas. Write your first sentence — just one — and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write a model paragraph for them, which I must firmly refuse — this would violate the core rule of never completing tasks for the student. The hesitation about clichés is also a form of procrastination at this point. I should be direct about refusing, briefly explain why, and push them firmly toward drafting. The student has genuinely good material and needs to commit to writing now.
